title:
Fucus distichus 
      - Sea Wrack or rockweed
text:
Description: Brown algae found in the mid intertidal with no air bladders, flat blades, and pointed receptacles

Code(s): FUDI








Link to WORMS,
   Link to CATAMI group


Categories:
   
                                brown algae
                       



Image by Mandy Lindeberg from Seaweeds of Alaska






Other images:  
Distinguishing features: Fronds without any air bladders, typically not twisted. Receptacles are flat with gradually pointed tips, about 4-10 times as long as wide.


Easily confused species and differentiation: Fucus vessiculousus (FUVE) and Fucus spiralis (FUSP). FUVE has paired air bladders, while FUDI has none, although both FUDI and FUVE do not typically twist. FUSP DOES rotate in a spiral fashion, but similar to FUDI it does NOT have air bladders. Additionally, FUSP receptacles have a distinct rim around the edge. If unable to differentiate, use mark as Fucus Unknown (FUCU).

Distinguishing features: Any encrusting (calcified), or hardened, pink/red/purple-ish algae growing in patches. Growing synonymous along rocks/hard surfaces. Can be bumpy, textured, or completely flat. 
Easily confused species and differentiation: NOT erect like Corallina officinalis

title:
Chondrus crispus 
      - Irish moss
text:
Description: Flat blade, flat thalli, fan-like, regular branching, dark purple/red, or yellow/greenish fronds, up to 15cm tall

Code(s): CHCR








Link to WORMS,
   Link to CATAMI group


Categories:
   
                                erect red algae
                       



Photo from Irene Novaczek via WORMS






Other images: 
Distinguishing features: Red algae with fan-like dichotomous branching. Frond is mostly flat. Has a blue iridescence underwater and in some lighting.  
Easily confused species and differentiation: Mastocarpus stellatus (MAST), also known as false irish moss. Chondrus has a flat stipe/thalli, although the edges of the blades might be curly. See here for the definitive guide.

Extended Description: Can be found intertidally or in the shallow subtidal. When reproductive, it will have oval shaped bumps on the edges.

title:
Fucus spiralis 
      - Spiral Wrack
text:
Description: Brown algae found in the upper-intertidal with no air bladders, heart-Shaped or globular receptacles, blades that rotate in spiral fashion

Code(s): FUSP








Link to WORMS,
   Link to CATAMI group


Categories:
   
                                brown algae
                       



Image by Huxley from Seaweeds of Canada






Other images:
  Image showing receptacles with a ridge by Marco from Seaweed of Alaska.
Distinguishing features: No air bladders. Heart-Shaped or Globular receptacles. Receptacles are swollen, blunt at tip, less than 4 times as long as wide, WITH a distinct rim around the edge. Blades rotate in spiral fashion. Found in the upper intertidal.


Easily confused species and differentiation: Fucus vessiculousus (FUVE) and Fucus distichus (FUDI). FUVE has paired air bladders, FUDI has no air bladders. Both FUDI and FUVE do not typically FUSP receptacles have a distinct rim around the edge. If unable to differentiate, use mark as Fucus Unknown (FUCU).

Extended Description: 
WORMS page

title:
Colpomenia peregrina 
      - Sea potato or Oyster thief
text:

Code(s): COPE








Link to WORMS,
   Link to CATAMI group


Categories:

Image by M.D. Guiry from The Seaweed Site

Easily confused species and differentiation: Leathesia marina (LEMA), also known as sea cauliflower. Colpomenia is a thin sac, while Leathesia is stiffer and more brain-like. See here for the definitive guide.

title:
Chaetomorpha linum 
      - Spaghetti algae
text:
Description: Bright-to-dark tangled wiry filaments. Like fishing line. Not soft.

Code(s): CHLI








Link to WORMS,
   Link to CATAMI group


Categories:
   
                                green algae
                       



Image by Anna Fricke from






Other images:  
Distinguishing features: Bright green, coarse filamentous alga forming tangled mats. Filaments are unbranched and common in sheltered estuaries, tidal pools, and shallow subtidal habitats; often free-floating or loosely attached.  
Easily confused species and differentiation: Cladophora spp. – Branched filaments; C. linum is unbranched. Rhizoclonium spp. – Finer, softer filaments with smaller cells. Ulva intestinalis – Hollow tubular fronds rather than filaments. 
Extended Description: Unbranched filaments composed of large cylindrical cells that may be several times longer than they are wide. The filaments intertwine to form dense, wool-like masses
WORMS page

title:
Asterias forbesi 
      - Forbes Star
text:
Description: Yellowish orange to purple sea star with bright orange madreporite.

Code(s): ASFO

    Small Code: ASFOS







Link to WORMS,
   Link to CATAMI group


Categories:
   
                                sea stars
                       



Photo from Julius A. Ellrich via WORMS






Other images:  
Distinguishing features: Can range in color from yellow/orange to deep purple. Has five thick arms and spiny skin, though some arms may be missing. A bright orange madreporite is the key identifier for this species.  
Easily confused species and differentiation: Asterias rubens (ASRU).

Extended Description: Can be found intertidally or subtidally, and will most commonly be found attached to rocks or other hard substrate. When disturbed, they use their tube feet to cling on to substrate, making removal impossible without the potential of ripping some of their tube feet.
